text: This could be once again, as it was in 2020. This could be the deciding factor in who controls the Senate. And a lot of it comes down to getting people reexcited. Yeah. Saying, "I know you're." I mean, the good thing here is they have some muscle memory for this, both as organizers and as voters. I need to say though, win or lose, Stacey Abrams has proven herself to be a transformational political character that does not change with the electoral loss. And I think it would also be a mistake. A lot of people are going to look at the state and they're going to say, not only did Stacey Abrams lose, but her playbook lost, right? Her argument that you need to invest over a period of time and go into communities that have ordinarily been neglected and put a big longitudinal investment there—that lesson needs to stick. There's still an argument for what she has been doing. And let's also remember that yes, she was a candidate for governor, but she also formed an organization. She's not just a candidate; she says she built it from the ground up. Yeah. From the from the ground up, and we're going to see that apparatus at play if indeed Georgia goes one hundred percent. Candidates come and go. That infrastructure. That's the long-term infrastructure. Yeah. It's forever.
